titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to Records of Ecumenical Work for Coordination and Documentation of Missions and World Christianity
Records of Ecumenical Work for Coordination and Documentation of Missions and World Christianity
titlestmt.author: Martha Lund Smalley

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday June 24, 2025 at 18:00

did.unittitle: Records of Ecumenical Work for Coordination and Documentation of Missions and World Christianity
did.abstract: This collection draws together documentation relating to ecumenical and interdenominational organizations and institutions that were established to coordinate and document aspects of the missionary movement and world Christianity. It complements other substantial archival collections held by the Divinity Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: While local interdenominational meetings had occurred earlier, the first large ecumenical missionary conference was held in London in 1888. This conference drew more than 1500 delegates, with representatives from 139 different Protestant denominations worldwide. The next major missionary conference was held in New York in 1900 with more than 200,000 attendees.

The rise of institutional organizations in the Protestant ecumenical movement can be traced to the early 20th century when the Federal Council of Churches of Christ in America was organized (1908) and the World Missionary Conference in Edinburgh gathered representatives of mission and church groups from around the world (1910). The World Missionary Conference's Continuation Committee, under the leadership of John R. Mott, sought to establish mission organizations headed by indigenous leaders. Members of the Continuation Committee headed to India, Burma, Malaysia, China, Korea, and Japan to gather information and hold meetings. The work of the Continuation Committee was interrupted by World War I, but it formed the foundation for the establishment in 1921 of the International Missionary Council.

World War II disrupted ecumenical activities throughout the world and the post-war period led in time to a decline in the mainline American Protestant churches that were the backbone of the ecumenical movement, but earlier efforts to develop organizations with indigenous leadership led to the establishment of interdenominational councils of churches throughout the world.

Efforts to coordinate activities of mission and church organizations often included a component of study and documentation of the environment and work of Christians throughout the world, and the religions and events surrounding them. As missions studies developed as an academic field, groups were formed to share knowledge and encourage investigation. The International Association for Mission Studies was formed in 1972. The Yale-Edinburgh Group on World Christianity and the History of Mission was founded in 1992 by Andrew Walls (University of Edinburgh) and Lamin Sanneh (Yale University). The Yale-Edinburgh Group's conferences and LISTSERV facilitate discussion and exchange of information about the development of world Christianity and historical aspects of mission, with special emphasis on the sources for documentation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection documents ecumenical and interdenominational organizations, institutions, projects, and events that sought to coordinate and document aspects of the missionary movement and world Christianity. Previously, many of these materials were treated as "Historical Records" in the Library's catalogued holdings; these records have been drawn together in order to provide a more coherent overview of available resources. The records in this collection complement other substantial archival holdings of the Divinity Library.

Much of this collection consists of printed or mimeographed reports, memos, brochures and other documentation of the work of the organizations and conferences represented. This material came to the Divinity Library in various ways. When the personal papers of John R. Mott were donated in the 1940s, many other files collected by Mott's operation were also given to the Library. Mott's crucial role in the establishment of ecumenical groups throughout the world from 1910 through the 1940s is documented both in his own papers (Record Group 45) and in this collection. Professors of mission at the Yale Divinity School, particularly Kenneth Scott Latourette and Charles Forman, were involved in ecumenical organizations throughout their long careers and directed rich material into the Library's holdings. It should be noted that the more ephemeral archival content of this collection is greatly complemented by the Library's cataloged holdings of reports, journals, pamphlets and books. The Library also holds collections of the official archives for a number of organizations documented in this collection, including the International Association for Mission Studies (Record Group 199) and on microform, the International Missionary Council and Conference of British Missionary Societies. In other cases the official archives of organizations represented are held elsewhere, such as the archives of the National Council of Churches of Christ in the USA held by the Presbyterian Historical Society.

While the documentation for many of the groups represented in this collection is fragmentary, there are substantive, detailed records for a number of organizations for the periods when Mott, Latourette and Forman were active. These include the Committee on Cooperation in Latin America, the Near East Council of Churches, the National Christian Council of Japan, the National Christian Council of the Philippine Islands, the National Christian Council of Siam [Thailand], and the Pacific Conference of Churches. Extensive documentation of the Council of Churches in Indonesia dating from 1957 to 1980 was provided to the Library by a former Executive Secretary of the organization.

Of particular interest in Series II, Conferences, are detailed records for the World Missionary Conference (1910) and its Continuation Committee, which was active until 1920. The 1910 conference solicited responses from missionaries in the field on various topics, including detailed descriptions of their work, and their perception of other religions, including Animist religions, Hinduism, and Islam.

Series III, Documentation projects and centers, includes records of two projects hosted by the Yale Divinity Library. The aim of the Ecumenical Leaders Index Project was to create a database of information regarding individuals, issues, and events connected with the ecumenical movement during the time period 1935-1960. Project staff surveyed all relevant manuscript and archival collections at the Yale Divinity Library to discover correspondence and papers of the individuals selected for inclusion in the Index. Database entry printouts provide biographical information for the ecumenical leaders and listings of available archival resources related to them. The Mission Studies Resource Development Project began in 1993 and over the next 18 months investigated the international base of research resources for mission studies and the study of world Christianity. It was sponsored by a grant from The Pew Charitable Trusts.

This collection excludes ecumenical groups that focused on educational and medical work as well as denominational and independent mission and church agencies. The "Historical Records" for these categories have been gathered into separate record groups.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is organized into three series:
Series I. Associations and coordinating organizations
Series III. Documentation projects and centers

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Records of Ecumenical Work for Coordination and Documentation of Missions and World Christianity (RG 342), Special Collections, Yale Divinity Library.

scopecontent.p: Location of meeting was changed from Hangchow to Madras [now Chennai] due to war conditions in China. The meeting was held in Tambaram, a section of Chennai.

Yale-Edinburgh Group on World Christianity and the History of Mission

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Andrew Walls (University of Edinburgh) and Lamin Sanneh (Yale University) founded the Yale Edinburgh group in 1992. The Group's conferences and LISTSERV facilitate discussion and exchange of information about the development of world Christianity and historical aspects of mission, with special emphasis on the sources for documentation. Its annual conferences have been hosted alternately by the Centre for the Study of World Christianity at the University of Edinburgh and the Yale Divinity School and co-sponsored by the Overseas Ministries Study Center. In 2025 the conference was held in the global South for the first time, at Mackenzie Presbyterian University in São Paulo, Brazil.

did.unittitle: Statement regarding first conferences; background paper by Lamin Sanneh "From Christendom to World Christianity"
Article by Prof. Dana L. Robert: "Naming 'World Christianity': Historical and Personal Perspectives on the Yale-Edinburgh Conference in World Christianity and Mission History"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Originally delivered as the keynote address at the 2019 Yale-Edinburgh Group conference, published in the International Bulletin of Mission Research , 2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The theme statements, or call for papers, of the conferences were prepared by the conveners of the Yale-Edinburgh Group - Andrew Walls and Lamin Sanneh for many years, followed by other representatives from Yale Divinity School and the University of Edinburgh Centre for the Study of World Christianity. Full conference programs and theme statements are not available for all years. Selected papers presented at the conferences are available in digital format at the Yale Divinity Library. Contact the Special Collections Librarian for more information.

did.unittitle: "From Christendom to World Christianity," at YDS (March) and "The Missionary Movement: Inter-continental Connexions" at Edinburgh (September)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Conferences included here were not sponsored by a particular mission agency or organization. They are primarily ecumenical in nature, gathering together on one-time or repeated occasions missionaries or people with an interest in missions. Search the Divinity Library's online catalog for additional instances of such conferences, such as the China Centenary Missionary Conference held in Shanghai in 1907.

did.unittitle: World Missionary Conference, Edinburgh 1910 and Continuation Committees
Commission I: "Carrying the Gospel to all the World" - questionnaire replies from China missionaries: A - U

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These documents, arranged alphabetically by name of missionary, describe their mission work in China.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This conference, chaired by John R. Mott, was one of various conferences sponsored by the International Missionary Council. Archives of the International Missionary Council, including documentation of its Research and Study Program, are available on microfiche at the Divinity Library.

scopecontent.p: For extensive additional documentation of student missionary conferences, see Record Group 42 - Student Volunteer Movement for Foreign Missions records, Record Group 46 - World Student Christian Federation records, Record Group 57 - Student Christian Movement in New England records, Record Group 239 - United Student Christian Council records and Records Group 247 - National Student Christian Federation Records.

scopecontent.p: For extensive additional documentation of student missionary conferences, see Record Group 42 - Student Volunteer Movement for Foreign Missions records, Record Group 46 - World Student Christian Federation records, Record Group 57 - Student Christian Movement in New England records, Record Group 239 - United Student Christian Council records and Records Group 247 - National Student Christian Federation Records.

Women's Foreign Missionary Jubilee, New Haven
Hosted or co-sponsored by the Yale Divinity School

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The aim of the Ecumenical Leaders Index Project, an endeavor funded by the Episcopal Church Foundation, was to create a database of information regarding individuals, issues, and events connected with the ecumenical movement during the time period 1935-1960. Project staff surveyed all relevant manuscript and archival collections at the Yale Divinity Library to discover correspondence and papers of the individuals selected for inclusion in the Index. Database entry printouts provide biographical information for the ecumenical leaders and listings of available archival resources related to them.

did.unittitle: Project description; list of individuals researched for database

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Mission Studies Resource Development Project began in 1993 and over the next 18 months investigated the international base of research resources for mission studies and the study of world Christianity. It was sponsored by a grant from The Pew Charitable Trusts. Stephen L. Peterson, Director of the Trinity College Library, was the project director. Project staff included Jonathan J. Bonk, Project Associate (Providence College and Seminary, Otterburne, Manitoba, Canada) and Joan Duffy, Research Assistant (Yale Divinity Library).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection contains documentation related to the investigations undertaken by the Mission Studies Resource Development Project. The Project employed three primary methods to garner information: mailed questionnaires, site visits, and consultations. Different questionnaires were constructed to be used with different populations: mission agencies, overseas church bodies, libraries, archives, and individuals.

did.unittitle: Correspondence - gathering information re. parties to be surveyed
Correspondence - Peterson site visits to Ghana and India
Correspondence - individuals interested in results of surveys
Survey tools - final questionnaires and drafts
Cumulative statistics for archival repository questionnaires
Completed questionnaires from libraries and educational institutions ("Factories")
Completed questionnaires from church bodies and mission agencies ("Mines")
Completed questionnaires from mission studies scholars ("Personal")
Final Project report - "Mission Studies Resources for the Future" by Stephen L. Peterson
Report on Ghana site visit by Jon Bonk
European Mission Resource Centers report by John Roxborogh
Articles related to mission study resources in International Bulletin of Missionary Research
Centre for the Study of Christianity in Islamic Lands, Kent, UK
East-West Center for Missions Research and Development, Seoul, Korea

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dossier No. 9 - "In Search of Mission"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Description of project. For papers related to NAMP available on inhouse server, consult with Special Collections Librarian
